slug: 美洲原住民的创世神话_乌鸦偷来了太阳
title: 美洲原住民的创世神话——乌鸦偷来了太阳

outline.concept: Trickster as transformer, not just troublemaker — in Pacific Northwest cosmologies, Raven steals the sun not for himself but to remake the world. His rule-breaking is world-creating: the chaos agent and the culture hero are the same figure.
outline.query: 打破规则 反而带来好结果 叛逆 创造 rebellion good outcome

outline.concept: Creation by transformation, not from nothing — the sun already existed but was hoarded by a chief. The world wasn't made ex nihilo; it was already there, incomplete and unjust. Creation is redistribution of what's been kept from everyone.
outline.query: 东西本来就有 被独占 重新分配 不公平 改造 renovation repurpose

outline.concept: Theft as sacred act — stealing the sun is heroic because it breaks hoarding. The moral valence is inverted: the real crime is keeping light to yourself, and the thief is the liberator. Possessiveness itself is the cosmic problem.
outline.query: 拿走别人的 分享 独占 慷慨 私藏 hoarding sharing liberate

outline.concept: Shape-shifting as cosmological method — Raven becomes a pine needle, is swallowed by the chief's daughter, is born as a human child, then cries until given the sun-ball. Crossing boundaries (animal↔human, outsider↔kin, small↔powerful) isn't decoration; it's the mechanism by which transformation actually happens.
outline.query: 伪装 身份转换 扮演角色 靠近目标 变通 shapeshift adapt role

outline.concept: The myth encodes real observation — ravens are genuinely manipulative, curious, and good at getting into things they shouldn't. The story maps actual corvid behavior onto cosmic function: what you notice about your neighbor is what you tell about your god.
outline.query: 观察动物 行为 聪明 好奇 乌鸦 联想 动物像人 animal behavior clever
